text: Okay, so onshore and offshore VAs. Offshore VAs can be very affordable for us here in Australia. One of the areas to be aware of with an offshore VA is that English is of course their second language, and so we need to be quite literal with how we give direction.
So if you think about all of the delegation versus abdication stuff I've been talking to you about over the last little while. Clear delegation to a VA is extremely important when they are offshore.

When English is a second language, even if they speak it very well, they can still miss a lot of the nuances, or we make a lot of assumptions as business owners. It makes it very hard for them to do their job well.
So it does require a large concentration from you as the business owner to ensure that you can get what you need out of them.

And I advocate very strongly for our offshore VAs to be given enough time to prove themselves. In Australia, we typically move somebody on pretty quickly, but an offshore VA, um, they need the time and the resources to be able to learn.

So you'd really want to have your systems tightly made and in place, and then you'd be training them consistently to get the results that you want. Look, an offshore VA can range from $10 an hour to $50 an hour. You'll get a matching skill set for what you pay.

If you're paying someone on the lower end, they're not going to stick around. They're going to go and find somebody that pays better. I'm an advocate for paying well and treating them like they're an onshore team member.

Uh, so Chloe, there's some bits and pieces here for you. Miles doesn't have a VA, he has Jackie and she costs a fortune. Nice work, Jackie. Um, Rhys has an onshore VA, she charges around $60 an hour. Um, it's done through another company, she does all of his invoicing and reconciliations, his BAS.

He's, um, she kind of takes care of his, um job management system, and he's working more on bringing her into the fold. Uh, Josh has previously had a quote for about $90 an hour but never went ahead. She was onshore.

I would want a business manager level for $90 an hour. Like, you would want some top shit for that. Um, I think that's quite ridiculous actually, but that is the gamut, you know. You're going from one end of the spectrum to the other. I think you would want something— somebody extremely efficient for $90 an hour.

There are some legal issues around offshore VAs, especially around privacy and accountancy. I have a friend who runs a psych clinic and she can't use offshore VAs for 90% of her work. That is correct.

Uh, last year there was a finding against an Australian company that had hired a VA, and it ended up that that VA was covered by Australian employment law. So after moving them on, there was a whole lot of back pay that needed to be done because they hadn't been paid appropriately.

They needed their sick leave, holiday leave, all of their entitlements, blah blah blah. I think the safest way to do it is through a company that can manage that for you. If you're looking for a full-time VA, I've got a company I can recommend, but they don't do anything more— less than full-time.

Um, so yeah, I think VAs can be great. If you've never had a VA before, I'd probably advocate for you to start with one that's onshore because there's a lot to learn about delegation when we have an offshore VA. That would be my word of caution.

If you've got any other experiences, please drop them into the chat. Chloe is also asking about tracking employee files, for example, their registration, police checks, contracts, all those kind of things. I would love for each of you to contribute and let us know how you track them.

They're using Google Drive currently, which works okay, but seeing what others do would be interesting. I use Google Drive for both of my businesses for this. Each team member has their own folder, and then I have alerts in my Asana or my calendar depending on the business to remind me to update.
You know, I've got alerts for their licensing, I've got alerts for their insurances, blah, blah, blah. That's how I do mine.

I don't have a lot of team to look after. If you were getting upwards of 10 team, I think you should start to look at other systems like Pretty sure Bright HR, this is the kind of stuff they can do. They have a filing system that is, so there's an urgent folder and there's a system that means that that gets completed or actioned every single day. And then there are other folders as agreed with, in this case it's Adam. So he has a folder for non-urgent inquiries, for administration questions.

Um, I think there's one there for client questions and he just works through those on a weekly basis. So everybody on the team knows that if they're in those other folders, it'll be answered on this day or this time. And the urgent ones get filtered through and, um, done every single day.
